questions.explanation: Book-back answer: Anthrax

questions.question: The Keyboard shortcut is used to copy the selected text
questions.explanation: Book-back answer: Ctrl+c

questions.question: The Keyboard shortcut is used to cut the selected text
questions.explanation: Book-back answer: Ctrl+x

questions.question: How many types of page orientation are there in Libre office Writer?
questions.explanation: Book-back answer: 2

questions.question: If the ruler is not displayed in the screen, _______ option is clicked.
questions.explanation: Book-back answer: View -> ruler

questions.question: The menu is used to save the document
questions.explanation: Book-back answer: file -> save

questions.question: Which of the following is the SI unit of temperature?
questions.explanation: The SI unit of temperature is Kelvin, as per the standard measurement system.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water in Celsius scale?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C under normal atmospheric pressure, as taught in the Heat and Temperature unit.

questions.question: Which of the following is a good conductor of electricity?
questions.explanation: Copper is a metal and a good conductor of electricity, as covered in the Electricity unit.

questions.question: Which of the following is a vector quantity?
questions.explanation: Force has both magnitude and direction, making it a vector quantity, from the Force and Motion unit.

questions.question: True or False: Photosynthesis occurs in the roots of plants.
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis occurs in the leaves, not roots, as studied in plant nutrition.

questions.question: Which of the following is a non-biodegradable material?
questions.explanation: Plastic does not decompose easily, making it non-biodegradable, from the Chemistry in Daily Life unit.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about sound is correct?
options: Sound speed is constant in all media
questions.explanation: Sound travels fastest in solids because particles are closely packed, as per the sound concepts.

questions.question: Which of the following is a method of vegetative propagation in plants?
questions.explanation: Cutting is a common method of vegetative propagation, as covered in Reproduction and Modification in Plants.

questions.question: True or False: Polymers are made up of small repeating units called monomers.
questions.explanation: Polymers consist of many repeating monomer units, as taught in the Polymer Chemistry unit.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a part of the human circulatory system?
questions.explanation: The circulatory system consists of the heart, blood vessels, and blood. The lungs are part of the respiratory system, though they work closely with circulation.

questions.question: Statement A: Sound can travel through a vacuum. Statement B: Light can travel through a vacuum. Which of the following is correct?
options: Both A and B are true
A is true, B is false
A is false, B is true
Both A and B are false
questions.explanation: Sound requires a medium to travel and cannot travel through a vacuum. Light can travel through a vacuum, as demonstrated by sunlight reaching Earth.

questions.question: True or False: The image formed by a plane mirror is always virtual and erect.
questions.explanation: A plane mirror always forms a virtual image that is erect (upright) and of the same size as the object.

questions.question: Which of the following is a chemical change?
questions.explanation: Rusting of iron is a chemical change because it forms a new substance (iron oxide). The other options are physical changes that do not form new substances.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a type of simple machine?
questions.explanation: A generator is a complex machine that converts mechanical energy into electrical energy. Levers, pulleys, and inclined planes are simple machines.

questions.question: Which of the following is the SI unit of length?
questions.explanation: In the International System of Units (SI), the standard unit of length is the metre.

questions.question: A force applied on an object can change its state of motion or its shape. (True/False)
questions.explanation: Force can cause a change in the motion or shape of an object.

questions.question: Which of the following is a compound?
questions.explanation: Water (H₂O) is a compound made of two elements, hydrogen and oxygen.

questions.question: Which of the following is a deficiency disease?
questions.explanation: Scurvy is caused by deficiency of Vitamin C.

questions.question: Which of the following is a physical change?
questions.explanation: Melting of ice is a reversible change in state and is a physical change.

questions.question: Who discovered the cell?
questions.explanation: Robert Hooke first observed cells in cork in 1665.

questions.question: In the five-kingdom classification, which kingdom includes unicellular organisms like bacteria?
questions.explanation: Kingdom Monera includes prokaryotic unicellular organisms such as bacteria.

questions.question: Which type of mirror is used as a rear-view mirror in vehicles?
questions.explanation: Convex mirrors provide a wider field of view, making them suitable for rear-view mirrors.

questions.question: Which of the following is a natural polymer?
questions.explanation: Cellulose is a natural polymer found in the cell walls of plants.

questions.question: Which acid is present in vinegar?
questions.explanation: Vinegar is a dilute solution of acetic acid.

questions.question: True or False: The atomic number of an element is equal to the number of neutrons in its nucleus.
questions.explanation: Atomic number equals the number of protons, not neutrons.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about light is correct?
options: Light travels in a straight line
Light is a form of energy
questions.explanation: Light travels in straight lines, through vacuum, and is a form of energy.

questions.question: Which of the following is a non-renewable source of energy?
questions.explanation: Coal is a fossil fuel and non-renewable, while solar, wind, and tidal are renewable.

questions.question: True or False: Sound can travel through vacuum.
questions.explanation: Sound requires a medium to travel and cannot travel through vacuum.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of a chemical change?
questions.explanation: Chemical changes result in the formation of new substances with different properties.

questions.question: The SI unit of length is ____.
questions.explanation: The SI unit of length is metre, as defined in the Measurement unit of Class 7 Samacheer Science.

questions.question: Which of the following is a method of vegetative propagation?
questions.explanation: Vegetative propagation includes methods like cutting, where a part of the plant can grow into a new plant, as studied in Reproduction and Modification in Plants.

questions.question: Which instrument is used to measure electric current?
questions.explanation: An ammeter is used to measure electric current, as explained in the Electricity unit.

questions.question: The angle of incidence is always equal to the angle of ______.
questions.explanation: According to the law of reflection, the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection, as taught in the Light unit.

questions.question: Which substance is commonly used as an antacid to neutralize stomach acid?
questions.explanation: Sodium bicarbonate (baking soda) is a mild base used as an antacid to neutralize excess stomach acid, as mentioned in Chemistry in Daily Life.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of length?
questions.explanation: The SI unit of length is the meter, as defined in the Measurement unit.

questions.question: What is the force that opposes motion between two surfaces in contact?
questions.explanation: Friction is the force that opposes the relative motion between two surfaces in contact.

questions.question: Which part of a flower develops into a fruit?
questions.explanation: After fertilization, the ovary of a flower develops into a fruit.

questions.question: Which vitamin is essential for good eyesight?
questions.explanation: Vitamin A is essential for maintaining good eyesight.

questions.question: What is the temperature range of a clinical thermometer?
questions.explanation: A clinical thermometer is used to measure human body temperature and has a range of 35°C to 42°C.

questions.question: Burning of wood is a chemical change.
questions.explanation: Burning of wood produces new substances like ash and carbon dioxide, making it a chemical change.

questions.question: Which organelle is known as the 'powerhouse of the cell'?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are called the powerhouse of the cell because they produce energy in the form of ATP.

questions.question: Which scientist proposed the five-kingdom classification?
questions.explanation: Robert Whittaker proposed the five-kingdom classification system in 1969.

questions.question: Which planet is known as the 'Morning Star' or 'Evening Star'?
questions.explanation: Venus is often called the Morning Star or Evening Star because it appears very bright in the sky.

questions.question: Which force is responsible for the motion of a ball rolling down a slope?
questions.explanation: Gravitational force pulls the ball downward along the slope, causing it to roll.

questions.question: The boiling point of water is 100°C at standard atmospheric pressure.
questions.explanation: Under standard atmospheric pressure (1 atm), water boils at 100°C.

questions.question: The angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection. This is known as the law of ______.
questions.explanation: The law of reflection states that the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.

questions.question: Nylon is a natural polymer.
questions.explanation: Nylon is a synthetic polymer, not a natural one.

questions.question: Which of the following are communicable diseases? (i) Malaria, (ii) Diabetes, (iii) Cholera, (iv) Cancer
questions.explanation: Malaria and cholera are communicable diseases caused by pathogens, while diabetes and cancer are non-communicable.

questions.question: Which of the following materials is a good conductor of electricity?
questions.explanation: Iron is a metal and a good conductor of electricity, while rubber, wood, and plastic are insulators.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of temperature?
questions.explanation: The SI unit of temperature is Kelvin, as defined in the Measurement unit of Term 1.

questions.question: Scurvy is caused by deficiency of Vitamin C.
questions.explanation: True. Scurvy results from a lack of Vitamin C, as discussed in Health and Hygiene (Term 1).

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the atomic structure is correct?
options: Electrons revolve in orbits around the nucleus.
Atomic number is the number of neutrons in the nucleus.
The mass number is the sum of protons and neutrons.
questions.explanation: Only statement D is correct. The nucleus contains protons and neutrons (not electrons), electrons revolve in orbits, and atomic number is the number of protons, not neutrons. This is from Atomic Structure (Term 1).

questions.question: Cell wall is present in animal cells.
questions.explanation: False. Cell wall is a characteristic of plant cells and is absent in animal cells, as explained in Cell Biology (Term 2).

questions.question: Identify the correct statements about electricity: (i) A closed circuit allows current to flow. (ii) An electric cell has two terminals. (iii) Glass is a good conductor. (iv) Fuse wire is made of copper.
questions.explanation: Statements i and ii are correct. Glass is an insulator, and fuse wire is usually made of an alloy with low melting point, not pure copper. From Electricity (Term 2).

questions.question: Which vitamin is produced by the skin when exposed to sunlight?
questions.explanation: Sunlight helps the skin produce Vitamin D, as discussed in Health and Hygiene (Term 1).

questions.question: Which part of the plant is responsible for reproduction?
questions.explanation: The flower is the reproductive part of a plant, as covered in Reproduction and Modification in Plants.

questions.question: True or False: Vaccination helps to prevent diseases by providing immunity.
questions.explanation: Vaccination stimulates the immune system to protect against specific diseases, as per Health and Hygiene.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of mammals?
questions.explanation: Mammals are characterized by the presence of mammary glands, as per Basis of Classification.

questions.question: True or False: Light travels in a straight line.
questions.explanation: Light travels in a straight line, which is a key concept in the Light unit of Term 3.

questions.question: Which of the following is a pure substance?
questions.explanation: Gold is an element and a pure substance, while milk, air, and sand are mixtures, as per the Matter Around Us unit.

questions.question: The process of fusion of male and female gametes in plants is called
questions.explanation: Fertilization is the fusion of male and female gametes, leading to seed formation, as per the Reproduction and Modification in Plants unit.

questions.question: Which of the following is a communicable disease?
questions.explanation: Chickenpox is caused by a virus and spreads from person to person, as per the Health and Hygiene unit.

questions.question: Heat flows from a body at higher temperature to a body at lower temperature.
questions.explanation: Heat always flows from a hotter object to a cooler object until thermal equilibrium is reached, as per the Heat and Temperature unit.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a criterion for classification of living things?
questions.explanation: Colour is not a reliable criterion for classification; cell structure, nutrition, and reproduction are key, as per the Basis of Classification unit.

questions.question: The image formed by a plane mirror is
questions.explanation: A plane mirror always forms a virtual and erect image of the same size as the object, as per the Light unit.

questions.question: Which of the following is used as an antacid?
questions.explanation: Sodium bicarbonate is a mild base used to neutralize excess stomach acid, as per the Chemistry in Daily Life unit.

questions.question: True or False: Sound can travel through a vacuum.
questions.explanation: Sound requires a medium to travel, so it cannot travel through a vacuum.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a part of the periodic table basics?
options: Elements are arranged by atomic number
questions.explanation: Not all elements are metals; there are non-metals and metalloids as well.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is correct about light?
options: Light travels in a straight line
questions.explanation: All these properties of light are covered in the Class 7 Samacheer Science curriculum.

questions.question: Which of the following is a method of reproduction in plants?
questions.explanation: All these methods are forms of reproduction in plants as per the chapter 'Reproduction and Modification in Plants'.

questions.question: Which of the following is a conductor of electricity?
questions.explanation: Copper is a good conductor of electricity, as taught in the 'Electricity' chapter.

questions.question: Which thermometer uses a bimetallic strip to measure temperature?
questions.explanation: A bimetallic thermometer works on the principle of different expansion rates of two metals joined together.

questions.question: Which of the following are agents of pollination? (A) Wind (B) Water (C) Animals (D) Light
questions.explanation: Wind, water, and animals are common pollination agents; light is not.

questions.question: Which of the following is a property of gases?
questions.explanation: Gases have no fixed volume or shape but are highly compressible.

questions.question: Which of the following are present in an animal cell? (A) Cell wall (B) Cell membrane (C) Nucleus (D) Chloroplast
questions.explanation: Animal cells have a cell membrane and nucleus but lack cell wall and chloroplasts.

questions.question: A substance that turns red litmus blue is:
questions.explanation: Bases turn red litmus blue; acids turn blue litmus red.

questions.question: Which subatomic particle has no charge?
questions.explanation: Neutrons are neutral particles found in the nucleus of an atom.

questions.question: In a flower, pollen grains are produced in the:
questions.explanation: The anther is the part of the stamen that contains pollen sacs producing pollen grains.

questions.question: Burning of wood is a physical change.
questions.explanation: Burning of wood is a chemical change because it produces new substances like ash and gases.

questions.question: A plane mirror forms a real image.
questions.explanation: A plane mirror always forms a virtual image that is upright and same size as the object.

questions.question: The planet known as the 'Red Planet' is:
questions.explanation: Mars appears reddish due to iron oxide (rust) on its surface.

questions.question: Which of the following statements are true about an electric circuit? (i) The bulb glows when the circuit is complete. (ii) An electric cell has two terminals. (iii) Current flows from negative to positive terminal. (iv) A switch is used to break the circuit.
questions.explanation: Statements (i), (ii), (iv) are correct. Current flows from positive to negative terminal, so (iii) is false.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a chemical change?
questions.explanation: Rusting of iron forms a new substance (iron oxide), so it is a chemical change.

questions.question: The state of matter that has a definite shape and volume is:
questions.explanation: Solids have fixed shape and volume due to strong intermolecular forces.

questions.question: Diseases that can be transmitted from an infected person to a healthy person are called:
questions.explanation: Communicable diseases spread through direct or indirect contact, e.g., cold, flu.

questions.question: When a force is applied on an object, what can change?
options: Its state of rest or motion
questions.explanation: Force can change shape, state of rest/motion, speed and direction.

questions.question: The cell membrane is found only in plant cells.
questions.explanation: Cell membrane is present in both plant and animal cells.

questions.question: Which of the following are causes of non-communicable diseases? (i) Malnutrition (ii) Virus (iii) Genetic disorders (iv) Lifestyle choices
questions.explanation: Virus causes communicable disease, while malnutrition, genetic disorders, and lifestyle choices cause non-communicable diseases.

questions.question: The five-kingdom classification was proposed by:
questions.explanation: Robert Whittaker proposed five kingdoms.

questions.question: Silkworm is reared for obtaining silk.
questions.explanation: Sericulture involves rearing silkworms for silk production.

questions.question: Which acid is found in lemon?
questions.explanation: Lemon contains citric acid.

questions.question: An atom of an element has 8 protons and 10 neutrons. Its atomic number is:
questions.explanation: Atomic number is equal to the number of protons. Here number of protons = 8, so atomic number = 8.

questions.question: The intermolecular force of attraction is maximum in solids and minimum in gases.
questions.explanation: In solids particles are tightly packed with strong intermolecular forces; in gases particles are far apart with weak forces.

questions.question: Which vitamin deficiency causes scurvy?
questions.explanation: Scurvy is caused by deficiency of Vitamin C (ascorbic acid). Symptoms include bleeding gums and weak joints.

questions.question: In an electric circuit, the symbol '⎓' represents:
questions.explanation: The symbol '⎓' (a long and a short parallel line) represents a single cell in circuit diagrams.

questions.question: Which cell organelle is known as the 'powerhouse of the cell'?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria produce energy in the form of ATP through cellular respiration, hence called powerhouse.

questions.question: According to the five kingdom classification, which kingdom includes unicellular eukaryotes?
questions.explanation: Kingdom Protista includes unicellular eukaryotic organisms like Amoeba and Paramecium.

questions.question: The angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection. This law is applicable for:
questions.explanation: The law of reflection (angle i = angle r) holds true for all types of mirrors, including plane, convex, and concave.

questions.question: Which planet is known as the 'Red Planet'?
questions.explanation: Mars appears reddish due to iron oxide (rust) on its surface, hence called the Red Planet.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of a monocot seed germination?
options: The cotyledon emerges out of the soil
The flower parts are in multiples of four or five
questions.explanation: In monocot seed germination, the cotyledon remains underground (hypogeal germination), whereas in dicots, the cotyledon often emerges above the soil.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of a dicot plant seed germination?
options: Seed leaves (cotyledons) come above the ground
The seed has only one cotyledon
The flower parts are in multiples of three
questions.explanation: In dicot plants, during epigeal germination, the cotyledons are brought above the ground. The other options describe monocot characteristics or hypogeal germination.

questions.question: True or False: The image formed by a plane mirror is always virtual and laterally inverted.
questions.explanation: According to the 'Light' unit in Term 3, plane mirrors produce virtual, laterally inverted images.

questions.question: Consider the following statements: (i) Sound cannot travel through vacuum. (ii) Light can travel through vacuum. (iii) Both sound and light are longitudinal waves. Which of the above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Sound needs a medium and cannot travel in vacuum, while light can. Sound is longitudinal, but light is transverse, so only (i) and (ii) are correct.

questions.question: True or False: All cells arise from pre-existing cells.
questions.explanation: This is a key principle of cell theory, as taught in 'Cell Biology' in Term 2.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a method of classification of organisms?
questions.explanation: Classification is based on morphology, anatomy, and genetics, not color, as per 'Basis of Classification' in Term 2.

questions.question: Which of the following is the SI unit of electric current?
questions.explanation: The SI unit of electric current is ampere (A). This is a standard unit covered in Term 2 Electricity chapter.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is correct regarding the image formed by a plane mirror?
options: It is virtual and laterally inverted.
It is real and of same size.
questions.explanation: A plane mirror always forms a virtual and laterally inverted image of the same size as the object. From Term 3 Light chapter.

questions.question: Which of the following is a thermosetting polymer?
questions.explanation: Bakelite is a thermosetting polymer, which cannot be remoulded on heating. This is from Term 3 Polymer Chemistry unit.

questions.question: Identify the incorrect statement about physical changes:
options: Mass of the substance is not conserved.
Composition of the substance remains the same.
questions.explanation: In a physical change, mass is conserved (Law of Conservation of Mass). The statement saying mass is not conserved is incorrect. From Term 2 Changes Around Us.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct unit of heat energy in the SI system?
questions.explanation: The SI unit of heat energy is joule (J). Calorie is also a unit but not SI. This is from Term 2 Heat and Temperature.

questions.question: If a body moves with a constant speed in a circular path, which of the following statements is correct?
options: It has a constant change in direction
It has no force acting on it
questions.explanation: In circular motion, speed is constant but direction changes continuously, leading to a change in velocity. The acceleration is directed towards the centre (centripetal acceleration) and is not zero. A force (centripetal force) acts on the body.

questions.question: A nurse uses a clinical thermometer to measure a patient's temperature. What is the typical range of this thermometer as mentioned in the 'Heat and Temperature' chapter?
questions.explanation: The clinical thermometer has a range from 35°C to 42°C, specifically designed for human body temperature measurements.

questions.question: Which of the following is the weak intermolecular force that exists between polar molecules like water, and is responsible for its high surface tension?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonding is a type of intermolecular force that occurs in polar molecules like water, leading to properties such as high surface tension and boiling point.

questions.question: Which of the following is the chemical symbol for the element 'Silicon'?
questions.explanation: The symbol for Silicon is Si. 'S' is Sulphur, 'Sl' and 'Sc' are incorrect notations.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about electric circuits: (i) An open circuit allows current to flow. (ii) A closed circuit does not allow current to flow. (iii) A fuse is a safety device that breaks the circuit when excess current flows. (iv) A resistor is used to increase the flow of current. Which of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanation: An open circuit breaks the flow of current, while a closed circuit allows it. A fuse is a safety device that melts and breaks the circuit to prevent damage. A resistor opposes the flow of current, not increases it. Hence only statement (iii) is correct.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a communicable disease?
questions.explanation: Diabetes is a non-communicable/metabolic disorder. Chickenpox, Tuberculosis, and Common cold are infectious and spread from one person to another.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs is correctly matched according to the 'Basis of Classification' chapter?
options: Mollusca - Soft body with a mantle
questions.explanation: Arthropoda are characterized by jointed legs. Porifera are pore-bearing animals. Mollusca have a soft body and often a shell (mantle). Echinodermata have spiny skin.

questions.question: True or False: The nucleus of a cell was discovered by Robert Hooke.
questions.explanation: Robert Hooke discovered cells while observing a thin slice of cork under a microscope. The nucleus was discovered later by Robert Brown in 1831.

questions.question: Which of the following is a thermosetting plastic used to make handles of utensils?
questions.explanation: Bakelite is a thermosetting plastic. It is a good insulator of heat and electricity and does not soften on heating, making it suitable for making handles of utensils. Polyethylene is a thermoplastic.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a vector quantity?
questions.explanation: Speed is a scalar quantity as it has only magnitude. Force, velocity, and weight have both magnitude and direction.

questions.question: Which part of the human body is primarily responsible for the sense of balance?
questions.explanation: The inner ear contains the vestibular system, which is responsible for maintaining balance.

questions.question: Which of the following diseases is caused by a deficiency of Vitamin C?
questions.explanation: Scurvy is caused by a deficiency of Vitamin C. Rickets is due to Vitamin D deficiency, Beriberi due to Vitamin B1, and Night blindness due to Vitamin A.

questions.question: Which of the following is a thermosetting plastic?
questions.explanation: Bakelite is a thermosetting plastic that cannot be remoulded on heating. Polythene, PVC, and Nylon are thermoplastics.

questions.question: In the context of the periodic table, which of the following statements about the modern periodic law is correct?
options: The physical and chemical properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic mass.
The physical and chemical properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic number.
The physical and chemical properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic radius.
The physical and chemical properties of elements are a periodic function of their valency.
questions.explanation: The modern periodic law states that the properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic number, not atomic mass.

questions.question: Statement 1: Sound cannot travel through a vacuum. Statement 2: Sound requires a medium for propagation. Which of the following is correct?
options: Both statements are true and Statement 2 is the correct explanation of Statement 1.
Both statements are true but Statement 2 is not the correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
questions.explanation: Sound needs a medium to travel, so it cannot travel through a vacuum. Statement 2 correctly explains Statement 1.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a part of the human digestive system?
questions.explanation: The trachea is part of the respiratory system, not the digestive system.

questions.question: Statement 1: All metals are good conductors of electricity. Statement 2: Copper is used in electrical wiring because it is a good conductor. Which of the following is correct?
options: Both statements are true and Statement 2 is the correct explanation of Statement 1.
Both statements are true but Statement 2 is not the correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
questions.explanation: All metals are good conductors, and copper is specifically used due to its high conductivity, so Statement 2 explains Statement 1.

questions.question: True or False: The sun is a planet.
questions.explanation: The sun is a star, not a planet.

questions.question: Which of the following is the largest planet in our solar system?
questions.explanation: Jupiter is the largest planet in the solar system.

questions.question: In vegetative propagation, new plants are produced from seeds.
questions.explanation: Vegetative propagation uses vegetative parts like stem, root, or leaves, not seeds. Seeds are used in sexual reproduction.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about plant cells is correct? (A) They have a cell wall made of cellulose. (B) They contain chloroplasts for photosynthesis. (C) They have a large central vacuole. (D) All of the above.
questions.explanation: Plant cells have a cellulose cell wall, chloroplasts, and a large central vacuole, so all statements are correct.

questions.question: The angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection. This is known as:
questions.explanation: The first law of reflection states that the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection.

questions.question: Diffusion is faster in solids than in gases.
questions.explanation: Diffusion is fastest in gases because particles move freely, and slowest in solids due to strong intermolecular forces.

questions.question: Which of the following diseases is/are caused by bacteria? (A) Tuberculosis (B) Malaria (C) Cholera (D) Both A and C
questions.explanation: Tuberculosis and cholera are bacterial diseases. Malaria is caused by a protozoan parasite.

questions.question: A student measures the length of a table using a meter scale and records 1.245 m. Later, using a vernier caliper, the same length is measured as 1.246 m. Which of the following correctly describes the accuracy and precision of these measurements?
options: The meter scale measurement is more accurate but less precise.
The vernier caliper measurement is more accurate and more precise.
Both measurements are equally accurate and precise.
Accuracy cannot be determined without the true value.
questions.explanation: The vernier caliper has higher resolution (greater precision) and typically gives a measurement closer to the true value (higher accuracy) compared to a meter scale.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about diffusion in matter is correct?
options: Diffusion is the movement of particles from a region of lower concentration to higher concentration.
Diffusion occurs fastest in solids due to strong intermolecular forces.
Diffusion in gases is faster than in liquids.
Diffusion does not require a medium.
questions.explanation: Diffusion is from higher to lower concentration, fastest in gases because particles move freely. Statement C is correct; others are false.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a communicable disease as per Samacheer Kalvi Class 7 Health and Hygiene unit?
questions.explanation: Measles, TB, and chickenpox are communicable (spread by pathogens). Diabetes is a non-communicable metabolic disorder.

questions.question: A laboratory thermometer and a clinical thermometer are used to measure temperatures. Which of the following statements is true?
options: The clinical thermometer has a kink to prevent mercury from falling back.
The laboratory thermometer can measure temperatures above 100°C.
Both thermometers use alcohol instead of mercury.
The clinical thermometer has a range of 0°C to 100°C.
questions.explanation: Clinical thermometer has a kink near the bulb to hold the mercury level. Laboratory thermometer has no kink, range -10°C to 110°C. Clinical thermometer range is 35°C to 42°C usually. Statement A is true.

questions.question: In an electric circuit, three identical bulbs are connected in series to a battery. If one bulb gets fused, what happens to the other bulbs?
options: The other bulbs continue to glow with same brightness.
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, current path is broken if any component fails, so all bulbs stop glowing.

questions.question: In a plant cell, which organelle is responsible for converting light energy into chemical energy?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and carry out photosynthesis, converting light to chemical energy.

questions.question: According to the five-kingdom classification, which kingdom includes organisms that are both unicellular and multicellular, autotrophic or heterotrophic, and have a cell wall?
questions.explanation: Protista includes unicellular eukaryotes, some autotrophic and some heterotrophic, many have cell walls (e.g., algae). Monera is prokaryotic; Fungi are heterotrophic with chitin cell wall; Plantae are multicellular autotrophs.

questions.question: Which of the following statements correctly distinguishes a star from a planet?
options: Stars twinkle, planets do not twinkle.
Stars produce their own light, planets reflect light from stars.
Stars are much farther away than planets.
All of the above are true.
questions.explanation: All three statements are correct: Stars twinkle due to atmospheric refraction, they emit light, and are far away; planets reflect light and appear steady.

questions.question: Bakelite is a type of synthetic polymer. Which of the following best describes its nature?
options: Thermosetting plastic, cannot be remoulded once set
questions.explanation: Bakelite is a thermosetting plastic (phenol-formaldehyde resin), which on heating sets irreversibly and cannot be remoulded.

questions.question: Which of the following substances is used as an antacid in daily life?
questions.explanation: Milk of magnesia (magnesium hydroxide) is a base that neutralizes excess stomach acid, acting as an antacid. Vinegar and lemon juice are acidic; soap is basic but used for cleaning.

questions.question: An atom of an element has an atomic number of 9 and a mass number of 19. How many neutrons are present in its nucleus?
questions.explanation: Number of neutrons equals mass number minus atomic number: 19 - 9 = 10.

questions.question: Which separation technique is most suitable for recovering pure common salt from an aqueous salt solution?
questions.explanation: Evaporation removes the volatile water, leaving behind the non-volatile salt crystals.

questions.question: Galileo's experiments on inclined planes led to the understanding that, in the absence of frictional force, a moving object will:
options: Continue moving with uniform speed forever
questions.explanation: This demonstrates the concept of inertia: an object in motion remains in motion with constant velocity unless acted upon by an external unbalanced force.

questions.question: Deficiency of which vitamin causes impaired blood clotting and increased bleeding tendency?
questions.explanation: Vitamin K is essential for the synthesis of prothrombin and clotting factors; its deficiency leads to hemorrhage.

questions.question: On a calm night after a hot day, the land breeze blows from land towards the sea because:
options: The land absorbs heat faster than the sea
The air above the sea is cooler and denser than the air above the land
The air above the land cools faster and moves towards the sea to replace the warmer rising air
The sea water evaporates rapidly at night
questions.explanation: At night, land cools faster than water. The cooler, denser air over the land moves seaward to displace the warmer air over the sea, creating a land breeze.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about changes around us: I) Melting of ice is a physical change. II) Rusting of iron is a chemical change. III) Burning of a candle is only a physical change. IV) Sublimation of iodine is a reversible physical change. Which of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanation: Burning a candle involves both melting (physical) and combustion (chemical), so statement III is incorrect. Statements I, II and IV are true.

questions.question: According to the five-kingdom classification system, organisms that lack a true membrane-bound nucleus are placed under:
questions.explanation: Monera consists of prokaryotic organisms that do not possess a membrane-bound nucleus, distinguishing them from other kingdoms.

questions.question: When an object is placed between two plane mirrors kept parallel to each other, the number of images formed is:
questions.explanation: Parallel plane mirrors reflect light repeatedly between them, producing an infinite number of images that fade from view due to light absorption.

questions.question: The different phases of the Moon observed from Earth are primarily caused by:
options: The rotation of the Moon on its axis
The varying relative positions of the Sun, Earth and Moon
The shape of the Moon's orbit around Earth
The shadow of the Earth falling on the Moon
questions.explanation: Moon phases result from changing illumination by the Sun as the Moon revolves around Earth, revealing different portions of its sunlit half.

questions.question: Unlike soap, a synthetic detergent can be used effectively for washing even in:
questions.explanation: Soaps form insoluble scum with calcium and magnesium ions in hard water, whereas synthetic detergents lather freely because their calcium and magnesium salts are soluble.

questions.question: True or False: The Murrah breed, known for high milk yield, is a variety of milch goat commonly reared in Haryana.
questions.explanation: Murrah is a famous breed of water buffalo, not a goat breed.

questions.question: Which of the following plastics is a thermosetting polymer that cannot be softened by reheating?
questions.explanation: Bakelite is a thermosetting plastic; once moulded and set, it cannot be remelted, unlike thermoplastics such as polyethylene, polystyrene and PVC.

questions.question: The SI unit of heat is joule.
questions.explanation: Heat is a form of energy and its SI unit is the joule (J).

questions.question: Which of the following statements about binomial nomenclature is/are correct? (i) First word denotes genus, second denotes species. (ii) It was introduced by Carolus Linnaeus. (iii) Both words are written in italics. (iv) The first letter of species name is capitalized.
questions.explanation: Statements i, ii, and iii are correct. In binomial nomenclature, the genus name is capitalized, not the species name.

questions.question: Which of the following are physical changes? (i) Melting of ice, (ii) Rusting of iron, (iii) Dissolving sugar in water, (iv) Burning of wood.
questions.explanation: Melting of ice and dissolving sugar are physical changes (no new substance formed). Rusting and burning are chemical changes.

questions.question: When a body moves in a circular path with constant speed, its velocity is constant.
questions.explanation: False. In circular motion, speed remains constant but direction changes continuously, so velocity (a vector) changes, resulting in acceleration.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a mode of heat transfer?
questions.explanation: Condensation is a change of state from gas to liquid, not a mode of heat transfer. The three modes are conduction, convection, and radiation.

questions.question: In a dry cell, the negative terminal is made of:
questions.explanation: In a dry cell, the zinc container acts as the negative terminal. Carbon rod is the positive terminal.

questions.question: All mammals lay eggs.
questions.explanation: False. Most mammals give birth to live young; only monotremes like the platypus lay eggs.

questions.question: The phenomenon of splitting of white light into its constituent colours is called:
questions.explanation: Dispersion is the splitting of white light into its component colours (VIBGYOR), as seen when light passes through a prism.

questions.question: Which of the following are diseases caused by bacteria? (A) Typhoid (B) Measles (C) Tuberculosis (D) Malaria
questions.explanation: Typhoid and tuberculosis are caused by bacteria. Measles is viral, and malaria is caused by a protozoan.

questions.question: Which of the following is a mixture?
questions.explanation: Air is a mixture of gases like nitrogen, oxygen, and others, as per Term I 'Matter Around Us'.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a physical change?
questions.explanation: Melting of ice is a reversible physical change, as per Term II 'Changes Around Us'.

questions.question: Statement A: Sound cannot travel through a vacuum. Statement B: Sound travels fastest in solids. Which of the following is correct?
options: Both A and B are true
Both A and B are false
A is true, B is false
A is false, B is true
questions.explanation: Sound requires a medium to travel and cannot travel through a vacuum. It travels fastest in solids due to the close packing of particles.

questions.question: True or False: The image formed by a plane mirror is always virtual and inverted.
questions.explanation: The image formed by a plane mirror is virtual and erect (not inverted).

questions.question: True or False: All metals are good conductors of electricity.
questions.explanation: Metals generally have free electrons that allow them to conduct electricity well, so the statement is true.

questions.question: Statement: 'Light travels in a straight line.' Is this statement true or false?
questions.explanation: Light travels in a straight line, which is the principle of rectilinear propagation.

questions.question: Statement: 'All metals are good conductors of electricity.' Is this statement true or false?
questions.explanation: Metals are generally good conductors of electricity due to free electrons.

questions.question: Which of the following is a non-biodegradable polymer?
questions.explanation: Polythene is a synthetic polymer that does not decompose easily, making it non-biodegradable.

questions.question: Statement: 'Sound cannot travel through a vacuum.' Is this statement true or false?
questions.explanation: Sound requires a medium to travel, so it cannot travel through a vacuum.

questions.question: Which subatomic particle is present in the nucleus and has no charge?
questions.explanation: Neutrons are neutral particles found in the nucleus of an atom.

questions.question: The acceleration of a body is 2 m/s² under a net force of 10 N. What is its mass?
questions.explanation: Using F = ma, mass = F/a = 10 N / 2 m/s² = 5 kg.

questions.question: Which thermometer scale has 100 equal divisions between the freezing and boiling points of water?
questions.explanation: Both Celsius and Kelvin scales have 100 divisions between freezing and boiling points of water (0°C to 100°C; 273 K to 373 K).

questions.question: The fuse wire in a domestic circuit is made of which material?
questions.explanation: Fuse wires are made of lead-tin alloy because it has a low melting point.

questions.question: The image formed by a plane mirror is always which type?
questions.explanation: Plane mirrors always form virtual, erect images of the same size as the object.

questions.question: Which monomer is used to make polythene?
questions.explanation: Polythene is a polymer made by repeating ethene (ethylene) monomers.

questions.question: The pH of a neutral solution is?
questions.explanation: A neutral solution has a pH of 7, neither acidic nor basic.

questions.question: Deficiency of iodine in the diet causes which disease?
questions.explanation: Iodine is essential for thyroid hormone production; its deficiency leads to goitre.

questions.question: Which planet is known as the 'Morning Star'?
questions.explanation: Venus is often called the Morning Star or Evening Star because it is very bright and visible around sunrise/sunset.

questions.question: Which kingdom includes organisms that are multicellular and have cell walls made of chitin?
questions.explanation: Fungi have cell walls made of chitin and are multicellular (except yeasts).

questions.question: Which of the following are chemical changes? (i) burning of paper, (ii) melting of wax, (iii) rusting of iron, (iv) breaking of glass.
questions.explanation: Burning of paper and rusting of iron are chemical changes because new substances are formed; melting wax and breaking glass are physical changes.

questions.question: Statement: 'All polymers are synthetic.' Is this statement true or false?
questions.explanation: False. Polymers can be natural (e.g., cellulose, rubber) or synthetic (e.g., plastic, nylon).

questions.question: Which of the following is a non-biodegradable substance?
questions.explanation: Plastic is non-biodegradable as it does not decompose easily by natural processes, unlike paper, vegetable peels, and cotton.

questions.question: Which of the following is a method of reproduction in plants where new plants are produced from vegetative parts?
questions.explanation: Vegetative propagation is a form of asexual reproduction where new plants grow from stems, roots, or leaves.

questions.question: An object moves along a circular path of radius 7 m. If it completes one full revolution and returns to its starting point, what is its displacement and the distance travelled respectively? (Take π = 22/7)
questions.explanation: After one full revolution, the initial and final positions coincide, so displacement is zero. Distance equals the circumference: 2πr = 2 × (22/7) × 7 = 44 m.

questions.question: Gaseous particles move very freely and fill the entire available space because they possess:
options: Strong intermolecular forces and low kinetic energy
Negligible intermolecular forces and high kinetic energy
High density and strong attractive forces
questions.explanation: In the gaseous state, particles have negligible intermolecular forces of attraction and very high kinetic energy, allowing them to move randomly and fill any container completely.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about atomic structure: Statement I: Electrons are distributed in discrete orbits called energy levels or shells. Statement II: The outermost shell of every atom can hold a maximum of 8 electrons. Statement III: The atomic number of an element equals the number of protons in its nucleus. Which of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement I and III are correct. Statement II is incorrect because the outermost shell of hydrogen and helium can hold only 2 electrons, and the maximum capacity of a shell is governed by the 2n² rule, not strictly 8 for all atoms.

questions.question: Which of the following diseases is caused by a protozoan and transmitted specifically by the bite of the female Anopheles mosquito?
questions.explanation: Malaria is caused by the Plasmodium protozoan and transmitted by the female Anopheles mosquito. Dengue and Chikungunya are viral diseases transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes, while Filaria is transmitted by Culex mosquitoes.

questions.question: A copper rod and an iron rod of equal length and diameter are heated at one end. The other end of the copper rod becomes hot faster than the iron rod. This is because copper:
options: absorbs more heat from the source
is a better conductor of heat than iron
has a higher specific heat capacity than iron
questions.explanation: Copper is a better conductor of heat than iron. Good conductors transfer thermal energy rapidly through the material, which is why the other end becomes hot faster.

questions.question: The process of burning camphor is a physical change because it disappears completely without leaving any residue.
questions.explanation: False. Burning of camphor is a chemical change. It reacts with oxygen to produce gaseous products such as carbon dioxide and water vapour. Sublimation of camphor (solid directly to gas without burning) is the physical change.

questions.question: Which cell organelle is responsible for modifying, sorting, and packaging proteins for secretion and also for the formation of lysosomes?
questions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus processes proteins received from the endoplasmic reticulum, packages them into vesicles, and forms lysosomes containing digestive enzymes. Ribosomes synthesise proteins, and mitochondria produce energy.

questions.question: A concave mirror is used as a shaving mirror because, when the face is placed between the pole and the principal focus, it produces an image that is:
options: real, erect and of same size
questions.explanation: When an object is placed between the pole and the principal focus of a concave mirror, the image formed is virtual, erect, and magnified. This enlarged image helps in shaving and examining the face.

questions.question: We observe different constellations during different seasons of the year. This is primarily due to:
options: The revolution of the Sun around the Earth
The revolution of the Earth around the Sun
The rotation of the Sun on its axis
The rapid movement of stars across the galaxy
questions.explanation: As the Earth revolves around the Sun, different regions of the sky face the night side of Earth at different times of the year, making different constellations visible during different seasons.

questions.question: Which of the following statements correctly distinguishes thermoplastics from thermosetting plastics?
options: Thermoplastics can be repeatedly softened by heating and remoulded, whereas thermosetting plastics cannot be remoulded once set.
Thermosetting plastics soften on heating and harden on cooling, and this can be repeated many times.
Thermoplastics have strong cross-linked structures that prevent them from melting.
Bakelite is an example of a thermoplastic.
questions.explanation: Thermoplastics (e.g., PVC, polythene) soften on heating and can be reshaped repeatedly. Thermosetting plastics (e.g., Bakelite) undergo permanent chemical cross-linking when heated and cannot be remoulded.

questions.question: Hard water does not produce lather easily with soap because it contains dissolved salts of:
questions.explanation: Hard water contains dissolved salts of calcium and magnesium (hydrogen carbonates, sulphates or chlorides). These react with soap to form an insoluble curdy precipitate called scum, preventing lather formation.

questions.question: Conduction is the transfer of heat through fluid movement.
questions.explanation: Conduction is the transfer of heat through solids by direct contact. Fluid movement involves convection.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about reflection are correct? (i) Angle of incidence equals angle of reflection. (ii) Incident ray, reflected ray and normal lie in same plane. (iii) Reflection can occur only from smooth surfaces. (iv) Virtual image is formed by plane mirror.
questions.explanation: Statements (i), (ii), and (iv) are correct. Statement (iii) is false because diffuse reflection occurs from rough surfaces.

questions.question: An electric cell converts chemical energy into electrical energy.
questions.explanation: In a cell, chemical reactions produce a flow of electrons, converting chemical energy to electrical energy.

questions.question: Which of the following are sources of milk? (i) Cow, (ii) Buffalo, (iii) Goat, (iv) Hen.
questions.explanation: Cow, buffalo, and goat are milk-producing animals. Hen is not a source of milk.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct unit of force in the International System of Units (SI)?
questions.explanation: The SI unit of force is the Newton, as defined in Samacheer Class 7 Term I Force and Motion chapter.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a physical change?
questions.explanation: Burning of paper is a chemical change, whereas the others are physical changes as per Class 7 Term II Changes Around Us.

questions.question: Statement A: Sound travels faster in solids than in air. Statement B: Light travels faster in water than in air.
options: Both A and B are true
Both A and B are false
A is true, B is false
A is false, B is true
questions.explanation: Sound travels faster in solids, but light travels slower in water than in air, as per Class 7 Term III Light and Term concepts.

questions.question: Which of the following diseases is caused by a virus?
questions.explanation: Influenza is caused by a virus, while the others are bacterial, as per Class 7 Term I Health and Hygiene.

questions.question: Statement: In a concave mirror, the image formed is always real and inverted.
questions.explanation: Concave mirrors can form virtual images when the object is very close.

questions.question: Which part of the human eye controls the amount of light entering?
questions.explanation: Iris controls the size of pupil and regulates light entry, as per Class 7 Term III Light.

questions.question: Which of the following substances is a natural polymer?
questions.explanation: Cellulose is a natural polymer, others are synthetic as per Class 7 Term III Polymer Chemistry.

questions.question: In the context of heat transfer, consider the following statements about a clinical thermometer and a laboratory thermometer: (i) The clinical thermometer has a kink to prevent the backflow of mercury. (ii) The laboratory thermometer has a stem without a kink. (iii) The clinical thermometer typically measures temperature from 35°C to 42°C. (iv) Mercury is the only liquid used in both types of thermometers. Which of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanation: Statements (i), (ii), and (iii) are correct as per the Samacheer Kalvi Class 7 Science (Term II, Heat and Temperature) textbook: clinical thermometer has a kink, laboratory thermometer does not, and clinical thermometer range is 35°C to 42°C. Statement (iv) is incorrect because some thermometers use alcohol or other liquids, though mercury is common.
